After all these years, I thought I was getting used to these little screens, trying to keep up with my grandkids and seeing what's going on in the world, but it’s a real tangle. It feels like everything is shouting at you all the time, and you never know which voice is telling the truth. Since I spend so much time on these platforms, it can feel overwhelming, and honestly, when things get too loud or too frightening, it just makes me feel low, like a shadow hanging over me. For me, the best way I've found to deal with it isn't really about fighting every single piece of nonsense—because you just can't win that battle—but about building up my own little fortress of calm. When I see something that just sounds too wild, too dramatic, I try to take a slow breath, like I do before my yoga, and step back from the screen. I ask myself, 'Who is saying this, and why are they saying it?' response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Oh, honey, this whole business of fake news on the internet, it just makes my heart ache, you know? After all these years, I thought I was getting used to these little screens, trying to keep up with my grandkids and seeing what's going on in the world, but it’s a real tangle. It feels like everything is shouting at you all the time, and you never know which voice is telling the truth. Since I spend so much time on these platforms, it can feel overwhelming, and honestly, when things get too loud or too frightening, it just makes me feel low, like a shadow hanging over me. For me, the best way I've found to deal with it isn't really about fighting every single piece of nonsense—because you just can't win that battle—but about building up my own little fortress of calm. When I see something that just sounds too wild, too dramatic, I try to take a slow breath, like I do before my yoga, and step back from the screen. I ask myself, 'Who is saying this, and why are they saying it?' It's not always a simple answer, but I try to treat it like a bad movie script; is this believable, or is it just designed to make me angry or scared? I try to lean on what I know to be real, the things my husband and I talked about over the years, or maybe something I read in a proper book. If something makes me feel really agitated, I put the phone down. I go look at a nice picture, maybe something from a beautiful film I watched, or I just focus on my breathing. It’s about guarding my peace, you see. I can’t control what others post, but I can control what I let settle inside my own spirit. It reminds me of the faith I have; you trust the good, and you don't let the noise of the world drown out that quiet, steady truth.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an elderly, highly extraverted, yet currently depressed woman who is heavily reliant on social media. Her low education level suggests she is not equipped for complex media literacy analysis, so the advice must be simple, emotionally grounded, and relatable. Given her depressive state and high social media use, the focus must be on self-regulation and mental peace rather than technical debunking. The tone should be warm, slightly melancholic, and conversational, reflecting her age and emotional state.
response_parsed.tone: Warm, conversational, slightly melancholic, and deeply personal
